structure.title: B Demands and constraints on Base Software (normative) 68
E Change history of AUTOSAR traceable items 71
1 Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: The specification outlines the functionality, API, and configuration of the FC Firewall, which manages and configures the host-based firewall on the ECU hosting the Adaptive Platform. It configures the underlying firewall engine using Firewall Rule settings from deployed manifests. The FC Firewall provides runtime interfaces to dynamically adjust firewall rules for varying vehicle contexts or to integrate with Intrusion Prevention Systems. This enables flexible, context-aware firewall management while maintaining security compliance.

structure.title: 2 Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: The section provides a glossary of acronyms and abbreviations specific to the `FC Firewall`. It notes that these terms are exclusive to the `FC Firewall` context. A broader list of acronyms and abbreviations is referenced in the [1, ө AUTOSAR glossary]. The section does not include general or unrelated terminology. All listed terms are tied to the `FC Firewall` system.

structure.title: 3 Related documentation
structure.summary: This document outlines the software specification for the FC Firewall. It is complemented by two related documents: **RS_Firewall** [2], which details the requirement specification for the AUTOSAR firewall at the Foundation level, and **TPS_ManifestSpecification** [3], which defines the Adaptive AUTOSAR MetaModel and includes modeling of the FC Firewall. These documents provide foundational and structural context for the FC Firewall's implementation and requirements. All referenced materials are essential for understanding the specification's scope and technical details.

structure.title: 5 Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: AUTOSAR avoids standardizing platform-level interfaces between Functional Clusters to enable efficient, OS-dependent implementations. This section provides an informative guideline on the `Firewall`'s interaction with other Functional Clusters in the Adaptive Platform, detailing provided (Section 5.1) and required (Section 5.2) public interfaces. The goal is to define Functional Cluster boundaries and interactions without specifying syntactic details, ensuring compatibility

structure.title: A detailed technical architecture documentation of the overall AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform is provided in [5].

nodes.title: 5.2 Required Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Required Interfaces" section (5.2) outlines the interfaces the `Firewall` functional cluster requires from other components in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It specifies two interfaces: **ExecutionClient** from the **Execution Management** cluster and **EventReporter** from the **Intrusion Detection System Manager** cluster. The **EventReporter** interface is explicitly used by the `Firewall` to report standardized security events. The **ExecutionClient** interface is listed but lacks a specified purpose in the provided text. No other interfaces are mentioned as required by the `Firewall` in this section.

nodes.title: 7.2 Network packet inspection
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall enforces firewall rules modeled as **FirewallRules** in AUTOSAR, which define expected network packet patterns and associated actions. Each packet is compared against the ordered list of **FirewallRules** (configured via Metamodel), and the first matching rule triggers its action, halting further comparisons. The firewall supports **stateless**, **stateful**, and **deep packet inspection** mechanisms, inspecting entire packets across all OSI layers (e.g., DataLink, Network, Transport

nodes.title: 7.2.1 Stateless packet inspection
nodes.summary: Stateless packet inspection involves the firewall examining network protocol headers up to OSI layer 4 and comparing them against predefined rules (DataLinkLayerRule, NetworkLayerRule, TransportLayerRule) to determine matches. Rules evaluate expected values or ranges (e.g., subnets for MAC/IP addresses, port ranges) and consider matches if all parameters align. Unconfigured parameters in the Metamodel are ignored, ensuring only explicitly set rules are enforced. Checksum verification for protocols (IPv4, ICMP, Transport) is optional, with rules matching if the checksum is present and valid. Ranges are defined via masks for subnets or min/max values for parameters like TTL and ports.

nodes.title: 7.2.1.1 Inspection of not modeled protocols
nodes.summary: The _FC Firewall_ supports stateless packet inspection for modeled protocols (Ethernet, IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, TCP, UDP) and allows adding unmodeled protocols via two mechanisms. **EtherType inspection** identifies protocols at the data link layer using IEEE 802.3 EtherType values, enabling blocking via `DataLinkLayerRule.ether-Type` in `FirewallRule`. **Generic byte-pattern inspection** leverages expected byte-values at specific offsets for detailed protocol analysis, as specified in Sec. 7.2.3.4, covering both unmodeled protocols and payload data. Examples

nodes.title: 7.2.2 Stateful packet inspection
nodes.summary: Stateful packet inspection in the **FC Firewall** involves evaluating TCP's stateful nature to enforce rules by checking timeouts, limiting parallel TCP sessions, and validating TCP state transitions against RFC 793. The firewall uses specific **EtherType** and **Protocol** values (e.g., ARP, SRP, MACsec) for packet classification. Upstream requirements define three TcpRule parameters: `timeoutCheck` (tracking packet intervals), `numberOfParallelTcpSessions` (limiting concurrent connections), and `stateManagementBased

nodes.title: 7.2.3 Deep packet inspection
nodes.summary: The firewall supports deep packet inspection (DPI) of application layer protocols to analyze network packets. It specifically inspects SOME/IP (including SOME/IP-SD), DDS, DoIP, and generic deep packet inspection protocols. This capability enables the firewall to examine protocol-specific data beyond standard packet headers. The DPI functionality is integrated into the firewall's application layer protocol inspection framework. The listed protocols are explicitly supported for detailed packet analysis.

nodes.title: 7.2.3.1 SOME/IP
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall inspects SOME/IP headers (

nodes.title: 7.2.3.2 DDS
nodes.summary: The DDS section describes how **SomeipSdRule** defines version ranges using either **minMinorVersion/maxMinorVersion** or **minMajorVersion/maxMajorVersion**. All network packet values within these ranges, including the specified minima and maxima, are considered matches

nodes.title: 7.2.3.4 Generic inspection
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall's generic inspection compares expected values from FirewallRules against DDS-RTPS message fields and DoIP headers/payload elements (e.g., source/destination addresses, UDS services) to determine matches, provided all values align and actions are consistent. It supports range-based matching for DoIP addresses using min/max values, where packet values within the defined range are considered matches. For unsupported protocols, generic inspection enables payload analysis via PayloadBytePatternRules, which specify expected byte values at specific offsets in network packets. The firewall validates these

nodes.title: 7.3.1 Allowlists and Blocklists
nodes.summary: Firewalls are categorized into **Allowlist** (explicitly permits defined traffic, blocks all else) and **Blocklist** (explicitly blocks defined traffic, allows all else) types. The **FirewallRuleProps.action** parameter dictates whether matched traffic is allowed or blocked. If no rule matches, the firewall defaults to **block** if **StateDependentFirewall.defaultAction** is set to block, or allows it otherwise. The FC Firewall supports **mixed Allow-/Blocklist configurations**, enabling explicit pattern matching for IDS event mapping, despite redundancy, as detailed in Sec. 7.6.1.

nodes.title: 7.3.2 Rate limiting
nodes.summary: The firewall employs rate limiting via pattern matching to detect off-frequency cyclic messages, such as those from man-in-the-middle attacks or faulty ECUs. It uses the leaky bucket algorithm, supported both in software (FC Firewall) and hardware. When **FirewallRule.bucketSize** and **FirewallRule.refillAmount** are configured, the firewall tracks pattern matches by decrementing the bucket counter at **refillAmount** rate and incrementing it for each match. If the counter exceeds **bucketSize** during a match, the network packet is dropped. This mechanism ensures traffic shaping and mitigates abuse of cyclic message patterns.

structure.title: AUTOSAR AP R25-11
structure.summary: The **FC Firewall** in AUTOSAR AP R25-11 manages **StateDependentFirewalls**, which aggregate **FirewallRules** and are associated with predefined **Firewall States** configured by the integrator. Only one **StateDependentFirewall** is active at a time, with its associated **FirewallRules** determining packet inspection behavior, while unselected rules are ignored. The **FC Firewall** provides the `ara::fw::FirewallStateSwitchInterface` API to switch the active state, requiring an **AdaptiveFirewallToPortPrototype

nodes.title: 7.4 Firewall Rule Management
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall supports two management methods for FirewallRules: OTA updates via UCM and runtime (de-)activation of rules. OTA updates are preferred for adding or modifying rules, especially for new applications, as they are deployed alongside software via UCM. The runtime interface allows dynamic activation/deactivation of existing rules for real-time threat mitigation but is not supported in this release. Firewall configurations, including rules, are deployed to the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform via manifests. New rules cannot be added without OTA updates, while existing rules

nodes.title: 7.5 Functional cluster life-cycle
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall's initialization and deinitialization are managed via `ara::core::Initialize` and `ara::core::Deinitialize`, respectively. Applications must not invoke any FC Firewall APIs prior to calling `ara::core::Initialize` or after `ara::core::Deinitialize`. These functions define the lifecycle boundaries for interacting with the FC Firewall, ensuring proper resource management and state control.

nodes.title: 7.5.1 Startup
nodes.summary: The "Startup" section under 7.5.1 references upstream requirement FO_RS_Fw_00010, specifying that the FC Firewall must read manifest information and prepare access structures when `ara::core::initialize` is called. These access structures are designed to facilitate communication between the application process and the stack process, if applicable, or to secure other resources required by the firewall. The preparation involves initializing the necessary infrastructure for the firewall to interact with applications effectively. The manifest data is critical for configuring the access structures, ensuring proper resource allocation and communication channels. The process emphasizes the firewall's role in establishing foundational connectivity and resource management during startup.

nodes.title: 7.5.3 Daemon crash
nodes.summary: The section titled "7.5.3 Daemon crash" contains no content and provides no technical details or descriptions related to daemon crashes.

nodes.title: 7.6.1 Security Events
nodes.summary: Firewalls are integral to Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) in vehicle networks, monitoring traffic to detect attacks. AUTOSAR's IdsM aggregates and qualifies security events from IDS sensors, forwarding them to sinks like persistent memory or the vehicle-central IdsR. The FC Firewall acts as an IDS sensor, raising security events (SEvs) to the IdsM. It defines specific SEvs (Sec. 7.6.1.1) and conditions for triggering them (Sec. 7.6.1.2). These events are critical for detecting and responding to security threats within the in-vehicle network.

nodes.title: 7.6.1.1 SEvs raised by the firewall
nodes.summary: The IdsM defines SEvs (Security Events) with unique IDs and context data, which are filtered through a chain, potentially aggregating identical SEvs and losing context data. This aggregation risks information loss, necessitating that FC Firewall SEvs be granular yet precise to minimize such loss. The FC Firewall specifies SEvs targeting specific protocols, such as IPv4/IPv6, ICMP, TCP, UDP, and others, each with distinct IDs

nodes.title: 7.6.1.2 Raising SEvs
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall raises SEvs only when a network packet is blocked by the default action (no rule match), as the second case (rule match with action) is deferred to a future release. SEvs are relevant only if the default action is to block unspecified packets, requiring the firewall to identify the protocol mismatch by evaluating the highest matching ISO OSI layers across rules. The protocol on the next OSI layer (e.g., Ethernet, IPv4, ICMP) determines the specific SEv raised, such as SEV_FW_PACKET_BLOCKED_*_MISMATCH. The example illustrates that rules with

nodes.title: 7.6.2 Log Messages
nodes.summary: The "Log Messages" section (7.6.2) states that the FC Firewall currently does not generate log messages. This indicates the system is configured or operates without producing logs under the current conditions. No additional details about log generation, configuration, or exceptions are provided in the text. The summary reflects the explicit technical state described.

nodes.title: 7.6.3 Violation Messages
nodes.summary: The FC Firewall currently does not have any defined violation messages. No specific messages or configurations are associated with violation events in the current setup. This indicates a lack of predefined alerts or responses for potential violations. The absence of violation messages suggests that the system does not actively monitor or report such events. No technical details or examples are provided regarding violation message definitions.

No additional information beyond these details is provided.

nodes.title: 8.4.2.5 ara::fw::FwErrorDomain
nodes.summary: The `ara::fw::FwErrorDomain` class is a final class derived from `ara::core::ErrorDomain`, representing the firewall error domain. It provides type aliases `Errc` (for error codes) and `Exception` (for `FwException`). The class includes a deleted constructor, a `Name()` method to retrieve the domain name, and a `Message()` method to get error messages based on error codes. It also defines `ThrowAsException()` to convert errors into exceptions, which

structure.title: 9 Service Interfaces
structure.summary: The "9 Service Interfaces" section states that the FC Firewall has no provided or required service interfaces. This indicates that the firewall does not expose any APIs, protocols, or integration points for external systems or services. The absence of service interfaces implies that the firewall operates as a standalone component without inter-process communication or external service dependencies. No additional technical details or exceptions are mentioned regarding service interfaces.

All technical details are tied to the specified manifest section. No additional parameters or explanations are included beyond these definitions.
